Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (21) - Requests
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle

In JavaScript, AJAX is one of those fundamentals you can't really skip — especially now that front-end/back-end separation is everywhere. So can Python do AJAX too? In this post we'll talk about Python's Requests library, and if you've ever used axios, you're going to feel right at home.
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (16) - pip

We've covered a lot of Python's basic syntax in the earlier chapters, so now I want to take a detour and talk about pip. It's something you'll reach for constantly in real-world development. If you already know npm, pip is going to feel pretty familiar — here's a rundown of the commands you'll actually use.
Learning Python From a JS Angle (9) — Container Types, Part 1

How did the BMI homework go? Whether you finished it or not, time waits for no one, so let's move on to the next topic. This time we dig into Python's container types — lists and dictionaries — and how they differ from JavaScript's arrays and objects. We'll also cover the fun stuff like get() and setdefault().
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (4) - Types and Variables

Okay, we're finally writing some actual Python. We've messed around for three days now, and if I don't start soon people are going to unsubscribe. Before we write anything, we kick off with the most basic thing there is: types. And as always, I'll compare Python and JavaScript side by side so the differences are easy to spot.
ThinkPHP Built-in Tags (1): Looping with volist, for, and foreach

We've actually been using a few of ThinkPHP's built-in template tags in earlier chapters, like volist, but I never stopped to explain what they actually do. So this kicks off a small series on the built-in tags. First up: the three looping tags. We'll cover volist (the powerful one), the simple for loop, and foreach — including the parameters like offset, length, empty, and key that make volist so handy.
Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 9 - Writing Your First Controller

Now that we understand the MVC architecture, it's time to start writing some code. The first thing we'll write is the Controller. But before we get there, there are a couple of settings we need to tweak so development goes a lot smoother. In this post we'll turn on debug mode, define a basic Controller, and walk through how ThinkPHP maps URLs to your controllers.
ThinkPHP Day 8: What MVC Actually Means (and Why You Should Care)

Before we get into the later chapters, I have to pump the brakes for a second and say sorry — we need to understand the MVC (Model-View-Controller) pattern first. This chapter walks through what MVC actually is, because the concept makes everything we build later way easier to follow. We'll go through what each letter means, how the flow actually works, and why splitting your code this way saves you from future pain.
